student_name: João Guilherme da Costa e Silva
hub_subtitle: Business English técnico para reuniões com fornecedores internacionais, normas e entrevistas

lesson.menu_title: Holding Your Ground -- Defending Technical Decisions Under Pressure
lesson.menu_desc: A Spanish signaling supplier claims the test delay on Line 4 was Motiva's fault -- late documentation approval -- and files an extension-of-time claim you do not accept. Tonight you learn to hold your ground without conceding: the language of a formal dispute (back-charge, schedule overrun, extension of time, to substantiate, burden of proof, to dispute, to rebut, without prejudice), the grammar that makes a position sound authoritative instead of anxious -- the mandative subjunctive: 'It is essential that the supplier submit the revised plan', 'We recommend that the team be notified' -- a real corrective-action letter you read the way the supplier reads it, and a live meeting where you acknowledge the concern, rebut it with a dated sign-off log, and close with a next step -- 40 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 16 -- Holding Your Ground
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- João Guilherme | Lesson 16 -- Holding Your Ground
lesson.grammar_point: the mandative subjunctive in formal requests (it is essential that the supplier submit / we recommend that the team be notified)

listenings.text: João, before you get on the call with the Spanish supplier this afternoon, let me tell you how I want you to run it, because the instinct under pressure is exactly the wrong one. They have filed an extension-of-time claim, and they are attributing the whole delay to us, to our documentation approval. The instinct, when a supplier says it is your fault, is to soften, to half-agree, to say well maybe we were a little late. Do not. The moment you concede a point you have not checked, the back-charge conversation is over and you have lost it. So here is the shape. First, acknowledge the concern, genuinely, once. Say you take it seriously and you have looked at it. That costs you nothing and it lowers the temperature. Then hold your ground on the facts. The burden of proof is on them, not on you, and you have the dated sign-off log, so use it. Do not raise your voice, do not get personal, just put the record on the table: the drawings were approved on the fourth, the units arrived six weeks after that, and the timeline attributes the overrun to the late units, not to us. Third, and this is the one people forget, stay diplomatic while you are being firm. It is essential that the tone stay professional, because you will work with these people again next year. You can dispute the claim and keep the relationship. And close with a next step, not a verdict. Propose that both sides review the log together before anyone talks about cost. That is holding your ground: firm on the facts, soft on the person, and always with a door still open.

Three things about defending a technical position when a supplier pushes a claim onto you, from someone who has sat through a lot of these disputes. First, separate the claim from the person. When Carmen says the delay is your fault, that is not an attack on you, it is a negotiating position, and it is her job to open high. The engineer who takes it personally starts defending his character instead of the facts, and the facts are the only thing that wins. So hear the claim as information about what she wants, not as an accusation, and your shoulders come down. Second, never concede what you have not verified, but always acknowledge what you have heard. These are not the same thing. Acknowledging costs nothing: I understand why you see it that way, let me look at the record. Conceding costs everything: yes, that was probably our fault. One keeps the burden of proof where it belongs, on the party making the claim; the other picks it up and carries it for them. And if a claim is genuinely unfounded, say so plainly but without heat: the record does not support that, and here is why. Third, the grammar of authority is quieter than you think. When you say, it is essential that the supplier submit the revised test plan before we proceed, you are not asking, you are stating a requirement, and the base form of the verb, submit, not submits, is what makes it sound like a norm rather than a favor. It is critical that the schedule remain unchanged. We recommend that the team be notified in writing. That is the language of a person who sets the terms, not one who reacts to them. Stay firm on the facts, warm on the relationship, and let the documented record do the arguing. You do not have to win the room. You only have to hold your position until the evidence does the work.

patterns.title: The mandative subjunctive -- how a formal requirement is built
cards: it is essential / critical / important that + subject + BASE verb

After expressions of urgency or importance, the verb stays in the BASE form for every subject -- no -s on he/she/it. "It is essential that the supplier submit the revised plan" (not submits). "It is critical that the schedule remain unchanged" (not remains). The base form is what makes it sound like a norm you are stating, not a favor you are asking.
recommend / require / insist / demand / propose / request that + subject + BASE verb

The same base form follows verbs of requesting and requiring. "We recommend that the team be notified in writing." "The client requires that every unit be inspected before shipment." "I insist that the log be reviewed by both sides." Notice BE, not is/are -- the subjunctive of the verb be is simply be for all persons.
the negative: subject + NOT + BASE verb

The subjunctive negative is just not + base -- no do/does. "It is essential that the supplier not ship the units before sign-off" (not "does not ship"). "We ask that the claim not be filed until the record is reviewed." The missing do/does is exactly what a Portuguese speaker adds by reflex.
It states terms instead of pleading

Compare "The supplier needs to submit the plan" with "It is essential that the supplier submit the plan." The first is your opinion about what should happen; the second is a condition you are setting. In a dispute, the subjunctive lets you be firm and impersonal at once -- you are not attacking the person, you are stating the requirement the contract already implies.

patterns.text: One rule runs through all of it: after these expressions the verb loses its ending and its do/does. It is essential that he submit -- not submits, not does not submit but not submit. We recommend that it be inspected -- not is inspected. The moment you hear yourself add an -s or an is, you have slipped back into the ordinary present and lost the formal, requirement-setting force. In a claim meeting, that force is the whole point: it is the difference between asking a supplier for a favor and reminding them of a term.

reading.rtitle: Corrective Action Request & Response to Extension-of-Time Claim -- Motiva Signaling to Ibersign S.A., Line 4 (adapted, written for João's package)
paras: Position. Motiva has received Ibersign's notice of a four-week schedule overrun on the Line 4 interlocking, together with an extension-of-time claim attributing the delay to Motiva's late approval of the interface drawings. Motiva acknowledges the concern and has reviewed it in full. We dispute the claim. The dated record does not support it, and the burden of proof for an extension of time rests with the party requesting it. This letter sets out our position and the corrective action we require, without prejudice to Motiva's rights under the penalty clause.

Findings. The interface drawings were approved on 4 March, a date recorded in the shared sign-off log and countersigned by Ibersign's own project engineer. The axle-counter units left the factory in Shenzhen six weeks after that approval, and two of the units delivered failed the incoming inspection carried out at our depot on 19 April. The timeline therefore attributes the overrun to the late delivery and the failed inspection, not to the drawing approval; there is no concurrent delay on Motiva's side. The claim that Motiva approved the drawings late is, on the record, unfounded. We are ready to substantiate every date in this paragraph from the log.

Corrective action and next step. Motiva requires that Ibersign submit a revised test plan within ten working days, and it is essential that the plan account for the two failed units and their firmware revision. We recommend that both parties review the sign-off log together before any discussion of a back-charge, and we propose, without prejudice, that the cost of the retest be shared pending that review. It is critical that the commissioning date remain unchanged; to that end, we request that the replacement units not be shipped until the firmware fix is confirmed in writing. Motiva remains committed to a constructive resolution and reserves its rights in full while that resolution is pursued.
reading.source: Motiva Signaling -- formal correspondence, Line 4 (fictional, written for João's package)

reading.text: Count the subjunctives holding this letter up: "Motiva requires that Ibersign submit", "it is essential that the plan account for", "we recommend that both parties review", "that the cost be shared", "it is critical that the date remain unchanged", "we request that the units not be shipped". Every one is a base-form verb after a word of requirement -- submit, account, review, be, remain, not be shipped. That is what lets the letter dispute a claim and stay coldly professional at the same time: it states terms, it does not plead. It is the exact move you produce tonight.

gist.prompt: What is this letter mainly doing?
choices: Accepting the supplier's extension-of-time claim and apologizing for the late drawing approval.
Acknowledging the supplier's concern, disputing the claim with a dated record, and setting out the corrective action Motiva requires -- while keeping the door open to a shared-cost resolution.
Cancelling the contract with Ibersign and demanding a full refund for the failed units.
Describing the daily duties of the signaling engineers working on Line 4.

items: Motiva accepts the supplier's claim that the delay was caused by Motiva's late drawing approval.
f
The opposite: "We dispute the claim. The dated record does not support it." Motiva acknowledges the concern but does not concede it -- that is the whole point of holding your ground.
The interface drawings were approved on 4 March, and the sign-off log was countersigned by Ibersign's own project engineer.
t

"The interface drawings were approved on 4 March, a date recorded in the shared sign-off log and countersigned by Ibersign's own project engineer." The record, not the argument, is what carries the position.
According to the letter, the burden of proof for an extension of time rests with Motiva.
f

"...the burden of proof for an extension of time rests with the party requesting it." The supplier requested it, so the burden is on them -- Motiva only has to hold its position until the record does the work.
Motiva requires that the supplier submit a revised test plan within ten working days.
t
"Motiva requires that Ibersign submit a revised test plan within ten working days..." Note the subjunctive: submit, not submits -- a stated requirement, not a request.
Motiva refuses any shared cost and insists the supplier pay the full retest under a back-charge.
f
"...we propose, without prejudice, that the cost of the retest be shared pending that review." The letter disputes the claim but still keeps a constructive, door-open next step.

items.situation: Carmen opens: "The delay is on your side -- you approved our drawings late." Acknowledge the concern without conceding the point.
tips: I understand why you see it that way, and I've taken it seriously -- let me put the record on the table before we go further.
I hear the concern, but I can't agree the delay was ours; the dated sign-off log tells a different story, and I'd like to walk you through it.

items.situation: You need to state a requirement formally, using the subjunctive. Tell Carmen the supplier must submit a revised test plan.
tips: It is essential that Ibersign submit a revised test plan within ten working days, one that accounts for the two failed units.
We require that the plan be submitted before we discuss any back-charge -- submit, not submits: it's a term, not a favor.

items.situation: Carmen attributes the overrun to your team. Rebut the claim with the evidence, staying calm.
tips: With respect, the record doesn't support that: the drawings were approved on the fourth, and the units arrived six weeks later -- the timeline attributes the overrun to the late delivery, not to us.
I have to dispute that; the claim is unfounded on the log, and the burden of proof for the extension sits with the party requesting it.

items.situation: The conversation is heating up. Keep the relationship while you hold your ground.
tips: To some extent I understand the pressure you're under, and I want a workable outcome -- but I can't concede a point the record doesn't support.
One could argue the schedule was tight for everyone; even so, it's essential that the commissioning date remain unchanged, and I'd rather solve this together than trade blame.

items.situation: You want to protect Motiva without closing the door. Reserve your rights and offer a next step.
tips: Let me put this on record without prejudice: we reserve our rights under the penalty clause, but I'd propose we review the sign-off log together first.
I recommend that both sides review the log before anyone talks about cost, and, without prejudice, that the retest cost be shared pending that review.

items.situation: Carmen asks for an extension of time. Give your position on the burden of proof, formally and firmly.
tips: The burden of proof for an extension of time rests with Ibersign, and on the record I don't see a concurrent delay on our side.
I can't grant the extension on what I've seen; if you can substantiate the claim from the log, I'll review it -- but the dated record is with us.

bank.label: Acknowledging without conceding
items: I understand why you see it that way, and I've taken it seriously -- let me put the record on the table.
I hear the concern; I just can't agree the delay was on our side, and here's why.
To some extent I understand the pressure you're under -- but I can't concede a point the record doesn't support.

bank.label: Stating a requirement (the subjunctive)
items: It is essential that the supplier submit a revised test plan within ten working days.
We require that the plan account for the two failed units before we proceed.
It is critical that the commissioning date remain unchanged; we ask that the units not be shipped until the fix is confirmed.

bank.label: Disputing and rebutting the claim
items: With respect, the record doesn't support that -- the timeline attributes the overrun to the late delivery, not to us.
I have to dispute the claim; it's unfounded on the log, and the burden of proof rests with the party requesting the extension.
Let me rebut that with the inspection carried out on the nineteenth -- the dates are all in the sign-off log.

bank.label: Holding ground while keeping the door open
items: We reserve our rights under the penalty clause, but I'd rather resolve this together than trade blame.
Without prejudice, I propose we review the log first and share the retest cost pending that review.
I recommend that both sides review the sign-off log before anyone talks about a back-charge.
